flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old girl, first febrile UTI, E. coli, defervesced 36h. Bladder US during admission shown.
flags.issue: Correct option mandates DMSA scan at 4-6 months after a FIRST UTI in an infant whose ultrasound was already done. RCH Melbourne CPG does not include routine DMSA in the post-UTI imaging cascade; DMSA is a NICE construct. RCH says renal US during illness for atypical UTI only, and 'most children do not require an ultrasound for a first UTI'.
flags.rch_says: Non-urgent renal US for recurrent UTIs only; US during acute illness for atypical UTI; infants <6 months with recurrent UTI or no antenatal scans get US. DMSA/MCUG not part of RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to: 'No additional imaging is required after the normal renal tract ultrasound; counsel parents on prompt presentation for any future febrile illness.' Demote DMSA option to a NICE-influenced distractor with a rationale noting RCH does not mandate DMSA.

flags.stem_snippet: 2-year-old, second febrile E. coli UTI in 6 months, prior US normal at first UTI.
flags.issue: Correct option is 'DMSA scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring'. RCH does not mandate DMSA. RCH would call this recurrent UTI and recommend a renal US (already done normal at first UTI; repeat US in the recurrent setting is the RCH path). Sources cite NICE NG224 alongside RCH, but RCH is meant to be the source-of-truth.
flags.rch_says: Recurrent UTI imaging = renal US; specialist referral; no routine DMSA in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to: 'Repeat renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral for recurrent UTI; no routine DMSA.' Move DMSA into a NICE-aligned distractor.

flags.stem_snippet: 3-year-old, second culture-proven E. coli febrile UTI; first 4 months ago.
flags.issue: Correct option specifies 'DMSA scan 4-6 months after most recent UTI'. RCH guideline does not include DMSA in the routine recurrent-UTI cascade.
flags.rch_says: Recurrent UTI = non-urgent renal US plus specialist consideration; DMSA not in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Replace correct option with 'Non-urgent renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral for recurrent UTI.' Note in rationale that DMSA is a NICE recommendation not adopted by RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 3-year-old, third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months'. Sources cite RCH but also NICE and CARI; correct option follows NICE not RCH.
flags.rch_says: RCH recommends renal US for recurrent UTI; DMSA/MCUG not part of the published RCH cascade.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite to 'Renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral'. Demote DMSA option.

flags.stem_snippet: 8-month-old, first febrile UTI, normal renal US, completing 10-day course on oral cephalexin.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months'. After a single typical UTI with normal US, RCH explicitly states 'most children do not require an ultrasound for a first UTI' and DMSA is not in the RCH cascade.
flags.rch_says: First typical UTI with normal US: no further imaging routinely required. DMSA not in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to 'No additional imaging required; parental education on prompt presentation for future fevers.' Move DMSA to a NICE-influenced distractor.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-month-old, first febrile UTI, antenatal scans normal.
flags.issue: Correct option arranges renal US during admission for a TYPICAL first UTI in a 5-month-old with normal antenatal scans. Stem doesn't establish atypical features; RCH does not mandate US during admission for typical first UTI in this age group when antenatal scans are normal.
flags.rch_says: Renal US during acute illness reserved for atypical UTI. Infants <6 months without antenatal monitoring or with recurrent UTI: US. With normal antenatal scans and typical first UTI: no routine US.
flags.proposed_fix: Clarify in stem whether antenatal monitoring was complete and whether atypical features are present, then make correct option 'no further imaging' if typical or 'renal US' if atypical/no antenatal scans. As written it overstates US indication.

flags.stem_snippet: 6-month-old girl, first febrile UTI, E. coli, responded to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'Renal tract ultrasound within 2 weeks'. After a typical first UTI in a 6-month-old with no atypical features and normal response, RCH does not routinely require US. Stem doesn't establish atypical UTI or absent antenatal monitoring.
flags.rch_says: Most children do not require US after first UTI; US reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, or <6 months with no antenatal monitoring or recurrent infection.
flags.proposed_fix: Either add atypical feature or absent antenatal monitoring to justify US, or change correct option to 'no routine imaging'. Also note empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate is not the RCH first-line; RCH lists cefalexin (cystitis) or gentamicin (IV).

flags.stem_snippet: 9-month-old, day 6 fever despite ceftriaxone, conjunctival injection, cracked lips - Kawasaki.
flags.issue: Not a UTI Q (Kawasaki) - swept into UTI bucket by keyword 'urine cultures sterile'. No UTI flag; mention only for triage accuracy.
flags.rch_says: N/A - Kawasaki management with IVIG + aspirin is RCH-aligned.
flags.proposed_fix: No fix needed; flagged as false-positive topic classification.

flags.stem_snippet: 14-month-old non-Aboriginal girl, 2 days fever, irritability, clean catch urine collected; await culture.
flags.issue: Correct option starts oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole empirically. RCH first-line empirical oral for paediatric UTI is cefalexin, not trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Trimethoprim is acceptable when sensitivities known but is not the RCH empirical recommendation.
flags.rch_says: Empiric oral cystitis: cefalexin 20 mg/kg (max 750 mg) TDS. Empiric oral pyelonephritis: cefalexin 25 mg/kg QID (<12 mo) or 45 mg/kg TDS (>=12 mo). Trimethoprim only after sensitivities.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TDS for 7 days while awaiting culture; review at end of course'. Demote trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole to a distractor noting it is reasonable only once sensitivities return.

flags.stem_snippet: 18-month-old, fever, leucocytes+nitrites+, 200 WCC/uL, choosing empiric oral antibiotic.
flags.issue: Correct option 'cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TWICE daily for 7 days'. RCH cefalexin dosing for pyelonephritis in >=12 months is 45 mg/kg TDS; for cystitis 20 mg/kg TDS. Twice-daily cefalexin is not an RCH-aligned regimen.
flags.rch_says: Cefalexin TDS for cystitis (20 mg/kg) or TDS/QID for pyelonephritis (25 mg/kg QID <12mo; 45 mg/kg TDS >=12mo).
flags.proposed_fix: Change dosing frequency to TDS (three-times daily) per RCH. Choose dose per cystitis vs pyelonephritis framing.

flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old, fever, catheter urine positive nitrites/leucocytes, choosing IV regimen.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'IV gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily and IV benzylpenicillin'. RCH empirical IV for paediatric UTI is gentamicin alone (with benzylpenicillin/amoxicillin added only if <3 months sepsis cover or listeria/enterococcus risk). At 4 months out of the <3mo bracket the routine RCH regimen is gentamicin monotherapy. Benzylpenicillin gives no UTI cover.
flags.rch_says: IV: gentamicin empirically; amoxicillin/benzylpenicillin only added in <3 months for listeria/enterococcus cover, not for UTI coverage itself.
flags.proposed_fix: If retaining combination regimen at 4 months, justify in rationale; otherwise simplify to 'IV gentamicin pending sensitivities'.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-month-old, fever 39, vomiting, choosing IV empirical regimen.
flags.issue: Same as ctb-p42-003: correct option adds benzylpenicillin/ampicillin to gentamicin at 5 months. RCH IV regimen for paediatric UTI >3 months is gentamicin monotherapy.
flags.rch_says: IV gentamicin alone for UTI >3 months; add ampicillin/benzylpenicillin only for sepsis cover in neonates and young infants.
flags.proposed_fix: Simplify correct option to gentamicin monotherapy at this age, or reframe stem as septic-pathway-not-UTI-only.
